title: URL Shortener (TinyURL)
description: High-availability service to map long URLs to 6-7 character aliases.
requirements: Key Generation Service (KGS) for offline key production
theory: The core challenge is generating unique, short keys without collisions. Using a 6-character Base62 string allows for 62^6 (~56.8 billion) unique URLs. To avoid runtime collisions and latency from DB checks, a 'Key Generation Service' (KGS) pre-calculates unique keys and stores them in a 'Used' vs 'Unused' table. When a write request arrives, the application server fetches a key from KGS, ensuring O(1) response time. For scaling, URLs are sharded by the Alias (Hash(Alias) % NumberOfShards).
components: Metadata DB (NoSQL like Cassandra for write-heavy capability)
bottlenecks: DB Read Throughput: Solved by Redis clusters.
KGS Scalability: Use Zookeeper to assign unique key ranges to different KGS nodes.
Data Growth: Implement TTL (Time-To-Live) for expired URLs.
diagramHints: 1. FLOW: Client -> Load Balancer -> Web Server. 2. LINK: Web Server -> KGS (to get a new key). 3. DRAW: KGS pulling from an 'Offline Key DB'. 4. LINK: Web Server -> Cassandra (storing long URL map). 5. SHOW: Redis cache intercepting read requests.

title: Pastebin Service
description: Text storage service focusing on large payloads and retrieval efficiency.
theory: Pastebin mirrors URL shortening but with much larger payloads. The architecture must separate Metadata (PasteID, CreationDate, Expiry, UserID) from the Actual Content (Raw Text). Metadata is stored in a relational DB for efficient querying, while Content is stored in an Object Store (Amazon S3). When a user requests a paste, the server fetches metadata to verify expiry/permissions, then retrieves the payload from S3. For frequent reads, a CDN or Redis cache stores the raw text.
bottlenecks: S3 costs: Use data compression (Gzip) before storage.
Database Sharding: Shard metadata by 'PasteID' to distribute read load.
Bandwidth: Huge pastes can saturate network; implement egress limiters.
diagramHints: 1. SPLIT: Metadata Path vs Data Path. 2. BOX: 'Object Storage' (S3). 3. DRAW: 'Cleanup Service' as a CRON job hitting the Metadata DB. 4. LINK: Analytics Service to a Message Queue.

title: Instagram / Photo Sharing
description: Massive scale photo upload and timeline generation system.
theory: In Instagram, generating the News Feed is the bottleneck. The 'Push Model' (Fan-out on Write) pre-generates feeds: when a user uploads a photo, it's injected into the timeline caches of all followers. However, for 'Hot' users (celebrities with millions of followers), a push model causes a 'thundering herd' at write-time. Solution: A Hybrid approach where standard users use 'Push' and Celebrities use 'Pull' (followers fetch their photos at read-time and merge with their cached feed).
components: Metadata Store (Cassandra - optimized for large writes)
Social Graph DB (Neo4j or sharded SQL)
bottlenecks: Metadata Sharding: Sharding by PhotoID causes cross-node queries for a user's feed. Shard by UserID instead.
Storage: Petabytes of images. Use deduplication and tiered storage (Hot/Warm/Cold).
Consistency: Eventual consistency is sufficient for 'Likes' and 'Comments'.
diagramHints: 1. BOX: 'Media API' and 'Feed API'. 2. PATH: Upload -> S3 -> Fan-out Workers -> Redis (Feed Cache). 3. LINK: Mobile App -> CDN for image binaries. 4. SHOW: Social Graph DB separately.

title: Facebook Messenger / WhatsApp
description: Real-time messaging with delivery guarantees and status tracking.
theory: Chat systems rely on Bi-directional communication (WebSockets). To maintain ordered messages in a distributed system, we avoid wall-clock timestamps and use monotonically increasing sequence numbers per conversation. For storage, Wide-Column stores (HBase) are ideal because they handle rapid, small writes efficiently and provide fast range scans for message history. Presence (Online/Offline) is tracked via heartbeats: if a client misses 3 heartbeats, it's marked offline.
bottlenecks: Fan-out on Presence: Updating 1000 friends about 1 user's status. Solved by status pulling or limiting push to active view.
Connection Scaling: Use a load balancer with Sticky Sessions or Consistent Hashing to keep users on the same Chat Server.
Storage: Billions of small messages. Use TTLs for transient chats.
diagramHints: 1. DRAW: Users connected via 'Sticky WebSockets' to Gateway. 2. LINK: Gateway -> Redis (for session info). 3. BOX: HBase for permanent storage. 4. PATH: Presence heartbeat -> Presence Service -> Database.

title: Twitter / X
description: Global distributed microblogging with real-time search.
theory: Twitter's architecture is read-optimized. The Home Timeline is pre-computed and stored in Redis. When a user tweets, it fans out to followers' Redis lists. The Search system uses an Inverted Index, but since tweets are short and real-time, the index must be rebuilt in memory (Earlybird service). Global ordering is ensured by Snowflake IDs, which incorporate high-precision timestamps, worker IDs, and sequences to remain unique and sortable without a central coordinator.
bottlenecks: Search Latency: Ranking billions of tweets in real-time. Solved by distributing search queries across many 'search nodes' and merging results.
Hot Shards: Popular topics or users hitting one DB partition. Solved by dynamic sharding.
Rate Limiting: Massive bot traffic protection.
diagramHints: 1. FLOW: Tweet -> Write API -> Fan-out -> Follower Redis. 2. LINK: Search API -> Distributed Inverted Index nodes. 3. BOX: Snowflake service. 4. SHOW: Celebrity Pull-path.

title: Universal Web Crawler
description: A system to index the internet with high efficiency and politeness.
theory: Crawling requires managing a 'URL Frontier'. The Frontier uses two levels of queues: Prioritization (based on page rank/utility) and Politeness (mapping hostnames to specific workers to ensure a delay between requests to the same site). Content deduplication is critical: we use Locality Sensitive Hashing (Simhash) to find 'near-duplicates' (e.g., same page with different tracking params). A Page-See component stores hashes of all crawled content to avoid re-indexing near-identical pages.
bottlenecks: DNS resolution: Millions of lookups. Solved by local DNS caches at every worker.
Spider Traps: Infinite URL loops. Solved by depth-limiters and link analysis.
Bandwidth: Multi-gigabit pipes required for distributed clusters.
diagramHints: 1. LOOP: Frontier -> Fetcher -> Parser -> Extractor -> Frontier. 2. LINK: Fetcher to DNS Cache. 3. BOX: 'Bloom Filter' for visited check. 4. PATH: Content -> Simhash Check -> Storage.

title: Yelp / Proximity Search
description: Geographical search service for points of interest (POI).
theory: Spatial querying involves dividing Earth's surface. QuadTrees recursively split the world into 4 quadrants; a leaf node is created when a quadrant contains < 500 POIs. This allows searching for nearby places in O(logN). Geohashing is another approach: it maps a 2D coordinate into a 1D string where longer prefixes mean closer proximity. For 'Nearby Friends' (moving users), QuadTrees are inefficient as rebuilding them is expensive. Instead, use a fixed Grid or Redis Geostacks which handle rapid location updates better.
bottlenecks: QuadTree Rebuilding: For millions of moving users. Solved by using Pub/Sub or Geohash-based sharding.
Hotspots: dense cities vs rural areas. Dynamic QuadTrees split and merge based on density.
Latency: Distribute QuadTrees across regional data centers.
diagramHints: 1. DRAW: A recursive grid (QuadTree structure). 2. LINK: LBS Request -> Geohash Calculator -> QuadTree Searcher. 3. BOX: 'POI DB'. 4. PATH: Client GPS -> Update Service -> Redis GeoSet.

title: Google Drive / Dropbox
description: File storage and synchronization service with versioning and high availability.
theory: Google Drive uses a chunk-based architecture. Files are split into 4MB blocks. If a file is updated, only modified blocks are re-uploaded (Delta Sync). To detect changes quickly, a 'Indexer' on the client calculates hashes of blocks. Conflict resolution usually follows 'last writer wins' or creates a 'Conflict Copy'. Metadata is stored in a relational DB for version control, while raw blocks go to S3/Object Store. Deduplication happens at the block level: if 100 users have the same 'Common Library' file, it's stored once.
bottlenecks: Upload Latency: Huge files saturate bandwidth. Use parallel uploads for different chunks.
Metadata Load: Billions of file versions. Shard metadata by 'FolderID' or 'NamespaceID'.
Connectivity: Mobile users losing signal mid-sync. Use resumable uploads.
diagramHints: 1. FLOW: Client -> Block Server -> S3 (Data). 2. LINK: Block Server -> Metadata Service (verify chunks). 3. BOX: Notification service pushing sync signals. 4. SHOW: DB Sharding by UserRoot.
deep_dive: Merkle Trees are the secret sauce. By comparing hashes of tree nodes instead of every byte, the server identifies exactly which 4MB chunk changed in a 10GB file in O(logN). For deduplication, we use 'CAS' (Content Addressable Storage) where the Block ID is the Hash of its content. This naturally prevents duplicate storage across the entire system.

title: API Rate Limiter
description: Protecting services from thundering herds, DDoS, and abusive users.
theory: Rate limiters act as a shield. The 'Token Bucket' algorithm is standard: tokens are added to a bucket at a fixed rate, and each request consumes a token. If the bucket is empty, the request is dropped (429 Too Many Requests). In a distributed environment, use 'Redis' to store counters. To avoid race conditions (two web servers updating the same count), use 'Redis Lua scripts' which execute atomically on the Redis node.
bottlenecks: Centralized Bottleneck: Calling Redis for every request adds latency. Use 'Local Caching' with periodic sync (Sync-to-Redis) for non-critical limits.
Clock Skew: Sliding window logs relying on system time. Use a central NTP or logical clocks.
Storage: Billions of user counters. Use Redis with eviction or TTL.
diagramHints: 1. DRAW: Client -> Rate Limiter Middleware -> Web Server. 2. LINK: Middleware -> Redis (Check/Increment). 3. PATH: Config Update -> Redis (Reload limits). 4. SHOW: Token Bucket replenishment logic.
deep_dive: The 'Sliding Window Log' prevents a burst of traffic at the edge of two windows (e.g., 59th second and 1st second). It stores timestamps of every request. However, it uses too much memory. The better approach is 'Sliding Window Counter', which approximates the count by combining the current window and the weighted average of the previous window.

title: Video Streaming (Netflix)
description: Global content delivery with adaptive bitrate and high resilience.
theory: Netflix architecture is split into the Control Plane (AWS for logic/API) and the Delivery Plane (Open Connect - their own CDN). Videos are encoded into hundreds of formats for every device. Adaptive Bitrate (ABR) means the player monitors network speed and switches between 360p, 720p, or 4K streams in real-time. To ensure 0 buffer, they push popular movies to CDN nodes inside ISP data centers, so data travels 'the last mile' only.
bottlenecks: Encoding Time: Taking 24 hours to encode 4K. Solved by parallelizing encoding of file chunks.
Network Congestion: ISP bottlenecks. Solved by placing CDN boxes directly in ISPs (Local caching).
Availability: AWS region outage. Solved by multi-region active-active deployment.
diagramHints: 1. SPLIT: Management (AWS) vs Delivery (CDN). 2. FLOW: Raw Video -> Encoding -> CDN Storage. 3. LINK: User Player -> Playback API -> Gets Optimal CDN URL. 4. SHOW: Multiple resolutions of the same video at Edge.
deep_dive: Chaos Engineering (Simian Army) is central. They intentionally kill servers and even whole regions to ensure the Control Plane is 'Anti-fragile'. For the video delivery, they use 'Predictive Caching': analyzing viewing trends to push a movie to a specific city's CDN *before* users even ask for it.

title: Distributed Cache (Redis Cluster)
description: High-speed key-value storage scaled across many machines.
theory: A distributed cache uses 'Consistent Hashing' or 'Hash Slots' to distribute keys. In Redis Cluster, there are 16,384 hash slots. Each node is responsible for a range. Nodes communicate via 'Gossip Protocol' to track health. To avoid data loss, each master has one or more slaves. Eviction is crucial: LRU (Least Recently Used) removes old data when memory is full. Cache Stampede is prevented by 'Locking' or 'Soft-TTLs' which trigger background refresh before expiration.
bottlenecks: Network Bandwidth: Gigantic values saturating pipes. Solved by data compression.
Hot Keys: Millions of requests for one key (e.g. celebrity profile). Solved by 'Local Caching' at the app level or 'Key Replication'.
Replication Lag: Slave being out of sync. Solved by synchronous replication (trade-off: speed).
diagramHints: 1. CIRCLE: Nodes arranged in a Ring. 2. PATH: Key -> Hash(Key) -> SlotID -> Node. 3. LINK: Master -> Slave (Replication). 4. BOX: App Server with small 'Local LRU Cache'.
deep_dive: Consistency vs Availability in Cache: Redis is usually AP (Available and Partition tolerant). It prioritizes speed. If a network partition occurs, writes to a minor side might be lost after recovery. For CP (Consistency), use 'Etcd' or 'Consul' which use the Raft consensus algorithm, but they are significantly slower.

title: Log Aggregation (ELK Stack)
description: Collecting, indexing, and searching billions of log lines in real-time.
theory: Logs are produced by thousands of nodes. 'Beats' (shippers) send logs to 'Kafka' (buffer) or 'Logstash' (processor). Logstash parses/transforms logs before sending them to 'Elasticsearch'. Elasticsearch stores data in 'Indexes' which are sharded by Date. Within each shard, Lucene creates 'Inverted Indexes' for fast searching. Kibana provides a UI to query this vast data.
bottlenecks: Write Throughput: Indexing is expensive. Solved by 'Bulk API' writes and turning off 'Refreshes' during high load.
Storage Cost: Logs grow indefinitely. Solved by 'Index Lifecycle Management' (Hot -> Warm -> Cold -> Delete).
Memory Usage: Elasticsearch is Java-based and memory-hungry. Use specialized heap tuning.
diagramHints: 1. FLOW: Server -> Filebeat -> Kafka -> Logstash -> ES. 2. BOX: 'Kibana' pointing at ES. 3. LINK: ES Shards mapped to Date ranges. 4. SHOW: Lucene segments inside a Shard.
deep_dive: Back-pressure is key. If Elasticsearch slows down, Logstash might crash or slow down the whole app. Kafka acts as the 'Shock Absorber', storing logs on disk until the indexing pipeline can catch up. To save space, we don't index 'Full Text' for all fields; we selectively choose fields for 'Keywords' vs 'Text'.

title: Notification Service
description: A centralized platform for multi-channel message delivery (Push, Email, SMS).
theory: A notification system must be reliable and scalable. It receives requests from various services, validates user preferences (Opt-out), and pushes messages into specialized priority queues. Workers consume these messages and call vendor APIs (Twilio, SendGrid, FCM). To prevent duplicates, each notification has a unique 'RequestId' which is checked against a cache before processing. Fault tolerance is added via automatic retry logic with exponential backoff if a vendor is down.
bottlenecks: Vendor Latency: Calling external APIs is slow. Solved by asynchronous processing via queues.
Message Bursts: Marketing campaigns flooding the system. Solved by rate-limiting the consumption rate.
Duplicate delivery: Network retries causing double SMS. Solved by distributed locks or idempotency tables.
diagramHints: 1. BOX: Notification API -> Validation -> Queues. 2. LINK: Queues -> Workers -> External APIs (FCM/Twilio). 3. DRAW: 'Preference DB' check before queueing. 4. PATH: Webhook -> Tracking Service -> Analytics DB.

title: Recommendation System (Netflix Style)
description: Scalable ML-driven engine for personalized content discovery.
theory: Recommendation engines follow a two-stage process. 1. Candidate Generation (Recall): Quickly narrowing down millions of items to ~100 potential matches using lightweight algorithms (Matrix Factorization, ANN search with HNSW). 2. Ranking: A more complex ML model (Deep Neural Network) scores these ~100 items based on user features, context (time, device), and item metadata. The results are then re-ranked by business rules (diversity, freshness) before display.
bottlenecks: Cold Start: New users/items have no history. Solved by content-based fallbacks (using metadata).
Inference Latency: Ranking models are slow. Solved by model quantization and caching top results for active users.
Data Drift: Models become outdated. Solved by continuous retraining pipelines.
diagramHints: 1. FLOW: User Context -> Recall Service -> Ranking Service -> Client. 2. PATH: User logs -> Kafka -> Feature Store -> Model Training. 3. BOX: 'Vector Database' (Pinecone/Milvus). 4. SHOW: A/B Testing switch at the end.
